metadata.title: Plausibility Auditor — Five Domains, One Audit Card

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The audit cards made the mechanism field the star for me: financial flagged 'no' because an implausibly high R-squared tripped anomaly detection, while legal came back indeterminate because citation verification lies outside the mechanism. I want to audit my own outputs. Help me: (1) run the CLI across the medical, legal, financial, logistics, and engineering domains on my outputs; (2) for each, name whether pattern recognition or anomaly detection fired; (3) write out the 'edge' column, the domain-specific correctness the audit cannot reach, so I know exactly what I still have to check by hand.

props.command: The audit cards made the mechanism field the star for me: financial flagged 'no' because an implausibly high R-squared tripped anomaly detection, while legal came back indeterminate because citation verification lies outside the mechanism. I want to audit my own outputs. Help me: (1) run the CLI across the medical, legal, financial, logistics, and engineering domains on my outputs; (2) for each, name whether pattern recognition or anomaly detection fired; (3) write out the 'edge' column, the domain-specific correctness the audit cannot reach, so I know exactly what I still have to check by hand.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

AI output sounds plausible. That is the problem. It is not fabrication in the sense of lying — it is pattern-completion that can produce text that reads correctly, cites real-looking numbers, and uses the right vocabulary for the domain. Plausibility auditing is the skill of naming what fired — pattern recognition or anomaly detection — and what the audit cannot reach. You cannot verify everything, but you can be precise about what you checked and what you did not.
beats.visual_intent: title card: plausibility vs correctness — the audit card concept — 'mechanism names what fired; expert supplies what it fires against'

beats.narration_text: Ask for the CLI. In the terminal: claude — write a Python CLI that accepts an output flag and a domain flag for medical, legal, financial, logistics, or engineering. Call the Anthropic API to return JSON with verdict, target, mechanism — pattern recognition or anomaly detection — domain knowledge required, and edge — what the audit cannot reach. Print a formatted audit card. Use claude-3-5-haiku.

props.command:
claude "Write a Python CLI: accepts --output (AI text)
and --domain (medical/legal/financial/logistics/engineering).
Call Anthropic API to return JSON: verdict (yes/no/indeterminate),
target, mechanism, domain_knowledge_required, edge.
Print formatted audit card. Use claude-3-5-haiku."

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. The audit is one structured API call. The mechanism field is the key: pattern recognition means the AI matched its training distribution; anomaly detection means it flagged something outside that distribution. Neither reaches domain-specific correctness — that is the edge.

props.code:
# plausibility_auditor.py — Claude Code output
import anthropic, json, argparse

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def audit(output_text, domain):
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model='claude-3-5-haiku-20241022',
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{'role':'user','content':
            f'Audit this {domain} AI output for plausibility.'
            f' Return JSON: verdict, target, mechanism,'
            f' domain_knowledge_required, edge.\n\n{output_text}'
        }]
    )
    # audit = json.loads(client.messages.create(...).content[0].text)
    return json.loads(resp.content[0].text)

beats.narration_text: Run it. Five domains. Five audit cards. Medical and logistics: verdict yes — plausible by the mechanism that fired. Legal: indeterminate — citation verification needed, which is outside the mechanism. Financial: verdict no — flagged — the R-squared value is implausibly high, anomaly detection fires. Engineering: yes, but load calculations were not checked. The edge column is not a failure; it names the boundary of what the audit reached.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_AuditCards: 5-row table, domain/verdict/mechanism/edge columns, financial row flagged CRIMSON

beats.narration_text: The edge column is truncated. Ask for a revision: expand the edge field to a full sentence, and add a requires-human flag that fires when domain knowledge required is true.

props.command:
claude "update plausibility_auditor.py:
  -> expand edge to full sentence
  -> add requires_human flag when domain_knowledge_required=true
  -> re-run all 5 domains"

beats.role_note: slate — fill with updated audit cards
beats.narration_text: Re-run. Four of the five domains carry a requires-human flag. The tool did not fail — it named what it reached and what it did not. That naming is the audit.
beats.visual_intent: updated 5-row audit card output with expanded edge and requires_human column

beats.narration_text: The mechanism names what fired. The edge names what it cannot reach. That distinction is what makes an audit card useful instead of vague. A verdict without an edge is not an audit — it is an opinion.
beats.visual_intent: summary card: mechanism vs edge distinction; 'a verdict without an edge is not an audit'

beats.narration_text: Your move: take one AI output from your workflow — any domain — and generate an audit card for it. Focus on the edge field. What did the audit not reach? That gap is where your human verification time needs to go.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) pick one AI output, 2) generate audit card, 3) act on the edge

That distinction is what makes an audit card useful instead of vague. A verdict without an edge is not an audit — it is an opinion. Your move: take one AI output from your workflow — any domain — and generate an audit card for it. Focus on the edge field. What did the audit not reach? That gap is where your human verification time needs to go.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Plausibility Auditor — Five Domains, One Audit Card] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
